ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for sweet plum sauce; A0CKN (fruit-based condiment) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sweet plum sauce condiment; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sweet plum sauce is approximately 30 g (about 2 tablespoons), used as a dipping sauce alongside roast duck, spring rolls, or wontons.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Plum sauce, ready-to-serve (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Chinese sweet plum sauce (苏梅酱) in USDA FoodData Central. Values adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for plum sauce (NDB 06164) and cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo condiment category data. Sodium value reflects the high-salt salted-plum base typical of Cantonese preparations. Input AI-generated values were reviewed against USDA plum sauce reference and retained where consistent; sodium (950 mg/100 g) is plausible for salted-plum-based sauce and is documented as an adapted estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Atkinson et al. (2008); analogous estimate for fruit-based sweet condiment sauces
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for sweet plum sauce (苏梅酱) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to fruit-based sweet condiment sauces with comparable sugar content (~40–45 g/100 g) documented in Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification convention, GI ≤55 is classified as 'low'; GI 56–69 is 'medium'; GI ≥70 is 'high'. GI 55 therefore falls in the 'low' category. Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: (55 × 46.5 × 0.30) / 100 = 7.67, rounded to 7.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (950 mg per 100 g) from salted plums and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit sweet plum sauce to no more than 1 teaspoon (approximately 5–10 g) per meal, as a 30 g serving contributes approximately 285 mg sodium — nearly 12% of the 2,400 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB and WHO.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar content (42.5 g per 100 g) and low-boundary glycemic index (GI 55)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit sweet plum sauce, as a 30 g serving delivers approximately 12.75 g of sugar and a glycemic load of 7.7, which can cause meaningful postprandial blood glucose elevation when combined with other carbohydrate-rich foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium and high added sugar content; negligible saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should use sweet plum sauce sparingly due to its high sodium (950 mg/100 g) and high sugar content, both of which are associated with elevated blood pressure and adverse lipid profiles; the negligible fat content (0.2 g/100 g) is not a concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet Plum Sauce (苏梅酱, Sūméi jiàng; Cantonese: Sou1 mui4 zoeng3) is a traditional Chinese condiment originating from Southern China and widely used in Cantonese cuisine. It is made by cooking salted plums with sugar, ginger, chili, and sometimes vinegar through a process of boiling and reduction, producing a thick, jam-like sauce with a smooth, slightly translucent amber or brownish-gold appearance. The flavour profile is a balanced combination of sweet and sour with a mild spicy undertone from ginger and chili. In Cantonese cuisine, it is the quintessential dipping sauce for roast meats such as roast duck (Siu Ngaap) and roast goose, where its fruity acidity cuts through the richness of the meat. It is also commonly served alongside deep-fried appetizers including spring rolls and wontons, and is a staple at dim sum restaurants and home kitchens across Hong Kong and Southern China. Nutritionally, sweet plum sauce is very high in sugar (42.5 g/100 g) and sodium (950 mg/100 g), making it a condiment to be used in small quantities. It provides minimal protein (0.5 g/100 g) and fat (0.2 g/100 g), with energy derived almost entirely from carbohydrates (46.5 g/100 g). A standard 30 g serving contributes approximately 55.5 kcal, 12.75 g sugar, and 285 mg sodium. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or hypertension in more than trace amounts. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, and gluten-free.
common_user_queries: What sauce goes with roast duck?
How much sodium is in plum sauce?
Cantonese dipping sauce for roast goose

confidence_reason: No exact entry for sweet plum sauce (苏梅酱) exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy plum sauce (NDB 06164) and cross-referenced with the AI-generated input values, which were internally consistent. Sodium value (950 mg/100 g) is plausible for a salted-plum-based sauce but represents an adapted estimate. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy plum sauce entry (NDB 06164) as no exact match for 苏梅酱 exists in any approved primary source. Input AI-generated values were retained where consistent with the USDA reference. 2. Sodium (950 mg/100 g) reflects the salted-plum base of authentic Cantonese sweet plum sauce and is documented as an adapted estimate. 3. saturated_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace per USDA plum sauce reference; this is a fruit-and-sugar-based condiment with no added fat. 4. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed zero — no hydrogenated oils in traditional preparation. 5. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: plant-based condiment, naturally zero. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg set to 0.0: plant-based condiment, naturally zero — no animal-derived ingredients. 7. vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: plant-based condiment with no UV-exposed ingredients; confirmed negligible. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for traditional sweet plum sauce not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. 9. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation not reported in available approved sources. 10. GI classification: GI value of 55 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney GI Database convention (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Previous iteration incorrectly applied 'medium'; corrected to 'low' in this iteration. 11. Duplicate portion entry in input (two identical 30 g entries) — deduplicated to single default serving of 30 g. 12. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 13.95) / 100 = 7.67, rounded to 7.7. 13. This is a Chinese / Cantonese dish, not a Singaporean dish; therefore Malay, Tamil, and Hindi multilingual fields are not mandatory per G6 rules and are left null.

Iteration 2: Corrected foodex2_match_type from 'analogous_estimate' to 'closest_parent'; gi_category set to 'medium' based on boundary interpretation.

Updated gi_source notes to explicitly state the classification boundary. Updated type2_diabetes key_factor to reference 'low-boundary glycemic index (GI 55)' for accuracy.
